section_what.p1: Electronic music with syncopated, broken rhythms. No predictable four-on-the-floor. Here drum loops are chopped, twisted and rearranged into an organic groove that hooks you viscerally.
section_what.p2: Born in the 70s in the Bronx. DJs like Kool Herc isolated the breaks — those sections where only the drums played — from funk and soul records. Those seconds of pure percussion became the seed of hip-hop, jungle, drum and bass and a whole universe of subgenres that keeps mutating.

items.title: THE ORIGINS
items.desc: Kool Herc, the Bronx, block parties. The 'merry-go-round' technique over funk breaks plants the seed of hip-hop and electronic breakbeat.

items.title: AMEN BREAK
items.desc: 6 seconds of drums from The Winstons. The most sampled loop in history. The foundation of jungle, DnB and half the internet.

items.title: UK RAVE CULTURE
items.desc: Illegal warehouses, pirate radios. Breakbeat conquers the British rave scene without asking anyone's permission.

items.title: BIG BEAT EXPLODES
items.desc: The Prodigy, Chemical Brothers, Fatboy Slim. Breaks fill stadiums, MTV and charts worldwide. The golden era.

items.title: NU SKOOL ERA
items.desc: Stanton Warriors, Plump DJs, Freq Nasty. The break gets polished: cleaner, fatter, built to destroy dancefloors.

items.title: THE RESURGENCE
items.desc: Fusion with UK garage, tech house, bass music. New producers, new labels. The break was never dead, it was just reloading.

history.title: History
history.subtitle: From the Bronx to dancefloors worldwide: origins, national scenes, crisis, resilience and the digital life of breakbeat.

origins.title: Origins: funk, soul and the birth of the break
paragraphs: Breakbeat is, at heart, dance music built on broken or staggered rhythms: drums feel chopped and syncopated, with more rhythmic play, compared to the straight pulse of much techno or house.

The name comes from drum "breaks" in funk, soul and early hip hop — moments where the percussion stands bare. DJs began isolating, looping, sampling and reshaping those fragments, and entire ways of making music grew from that.

In the Bronx in the early 1970s, DJ Kool Herc stretched those moments using two copies of the same record — the merry-go-round technique. That practice is the seed of modern hip hop and, later, of club breakbeat as we know it.

The same family tree includes the Amen break: a few seconds of drums from The Winstons that became one of history’s most sampled loops, underpinning jungle, drum & bass and huge swathes of online culture.
At this stage there is still no single "breakbeat" club genre — there are breaks as material, as technique and as sampling culture, ready to mutate into full genres.

uk_breakbeat.title: United Kingdom: rave, hardcore and the break as backbone
paragraphs: In the late 1980s acid house and American techno hit the UK, but they did not stay "pure": they collided with hip hop, electro, Jamaican dub and soundsystem culture, synth-pop, EBM and new beat. From that storm came breakbeat hardcore, early jungle and illegal rave culture — warehouses, pirates, fields.

London concentrated record shops, pirate radio and clubs, but British rave was not London-only: Manchester, Bristol, Sheffield and other cities mattered equally. The Prodigy, for example, did not appear from nowhere — they emerged from a broth where club kicks, chopped breaks, ragga samples, rave pianos and almost-punk attitude already mingled.

Names worth pulling on: Shut Up and Dance, SL2, Altern-8, N-Joi, The Ragga Twins, 808 State, Renegade Soundwave, The KLF, Acen; toward jungle and rougher sonics, 4hero, Goldie a little later, Reinforced Records as a hub.

Historically the US supplied many languages (house, techno, hip hop); Jamaica supplied bass culture and mixing philosophy; the UK acted as a huge amplifier — reinterpreting, radicalising and turning dance electronics into mass scenes. That is why breakbeat weighs so heavily in British identity: not because "everything" in England is breakbeat, but because broken rhythm occupies far more narrative space than in the classic US club story.
From that wave came big beat, nu skool breaks and the worldwide export of a sound tied to dancefloor energy, bass and drums less straight than four-on-the-floor.

us_breaks.title: United States: breaks on a different map
paragraphs: The practice of the break is shared — Bronx, funk, sampling — but the US club story unfolded differently: the dominant arc organised more clearly around house and techno on a 4x4 grid, while breakbeat took regional, less "central" paths in mainstream press.

Strong scenes always existed: electro, Miami bass, Florida breaks (Miami / south Florida), funky breaks, West Coast flavours — music with breaks, bass and local rave energy, but more fragmented than the UK arc from hardcore to jungle to DnB.
The lesson for reading maps: "British breakbeat" and "American breaks" are not two species born apart, but two ways the same germ grew in different ecosystems.

andalusian.title: Andalusia: a mass phenomenon and living memory
paragraphs: In Andalusia breakbeat was not just another scene: from roughly 1992 to 2002 it swelled into a highly visible social and generational wave — radio, mega-parties, promoters, weekend identity. Documentaries and cultural journalism have framed it as unusual in southern Europe for a genre that elsewhere often lived as a niche.

The film "Break Nation: The electronics that danced Andalusia" (2023, David Pareja) traces that cycle of euphoria and fracture; it is a solid editorial anchor for treating Andalusia as its own chapter in global breakbeat history.

The turning point often cited is the night at the Martín Carpena arena in Málaga (2 March 2002): overcrowding, crushing and, most seriously, fatalities reported in the press in connection with substance use. Afterward the sound and scene were heavily stigmatised; events were cancelled and many institutions turned away from that culture.

It is vital to nuance: it was not a total blackout. The scene went latent — DJs, producers, memories, smaller circuits — until the symbolic "resurrection" headlines and works like the film above can describe today.
In parallel, breakbeat’s fall as the club’s "headline" genre was global: in Andalusia a brutal local shock coincided with a wider shift toward other bass and electronic sounds.

australian.title: Australia: continuity, festivals and diaspora
paragraphs: Beyond the UK and US, Australia is one territory you can cite with confidence: its own scene, recurring events and artists who later leap into the international circuit.
A paradigm case: Breakfest in Perth, active for decades as a meeting point for breakbeat, drum & bass, hip hop and related electronics — proof that breaks there were infrastructure, not a footnote.

Pendulum formed in Perth before relocating to London: a sign that the Australian broth produced musicians who could project globally. The Australian scene is usually described as solid and continuous, unlike the almost "total pop" peak Andalusian breakbeat reached.

rise_decline_revival.title: Peak, loss of centre and resistance
paragraphs: From the mid-2000s into the early 2010s breakbeat stopped being club mainstream’s central umbrella: electro house, dubstep, house/techno branches and other bass styles took space. Rather than vanishing, the genre left the centre of the room.

In London, awards and institutions such as Breakspoll (International Breakbeat Awards) passed through Fabric in the 2000s and later other venues and cities — a sign of a living scene but also of relocation and less uniform media focus.

Producers and DJs document the transition — for example shifts toward electro-house in releases of the era. At the same time, figures such as Krafty Kuts or Lady Waks kept the flame through sets, radio, podcasts and online presence: culture survived as a network of booths and communities, not only as commercial charts.

Telling the story fairly means separating those who stayed in breaks; those who moved stylistically inside electronic music; and those whose production quietened for years without leaving the scene entirely.

digital_era.title: Digital era: record shops then and platforms now
paragraphs: Before Beatport and algorithms, finding breaks meant specialist shops, promos and anonymous white labels, dubplates and radio — pirate or legal — that turned a promo into a "hot" tune. Camden, labels such as Finger Lickin’, Bar Vinyl culture and London’s shop ecosystem belong to that infrastructure memory, not only to star artists.

Today platforms such as Beatport keep categories like "Breaks / Breakbeat / UK Bass": proof of commercial and DJ continuity, even if the umbrella now bundles sensibilities that once felt more separate — continuity with change, not a sealed museum.

YouTube, Mixcloud and SoundCloud inherited part of radio and mixtape culture: breakbeat remains niche culture with real searches, new charts and producers remixing the language with UK bass and current hybrids.
